Using this configuration with other types of SFTP/FTP hosts can result in duplicate file processing.</div></div></div></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Skip empty files</p></td><td><p>Skip empty files during processing.</p></td><td><p>No</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Polling Frequency</p></td><td><p>Frequency at which the server checks the source path for new files. The default value is <code>1 minute</code>.</p></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Size check wait time</p></td><td><p>Wait time, in seconds, between size checks to determine if a file is ready for processing. The default value is <code>1 second</code>. This allows a file write to complete before processing.<br><br>Mule performs two size checks by waiting the amount of time specified in the <code>Size check wait time</code> field. If both checks return the same value, the file is ready to process.<br><br>To disable this feature, specify a negative number or omit the value.</p></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Support Format type</p></td><td><p>Indicates whether the endpoint supports EDI messages (EDIFACT, X12) or non-EDI messages (JSON, XML).</p></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Associated message type</p></td><td><p>(Applies only to endpoints that support non-EDI formats) Message type to associate with the endpoint. If you select a message type, Partner Manager processes files received via the endpoint as the selected message type, regardless of the file naming convention.</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Use hyphen (-) as the secondary file name delimiter to determine non-EDI message type</p></td><td><p>Lets you use a hyphen as the secondary file name delimiter to determine the message type from the messageâs file name. Only shows if the supported format type is non-EDI.</p></td><td><p>No</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Associated reference identifier</p></td><td><p>(Applies only to endpoints that support non-EDI formats that are owned by partners or third-party connections.) Partner information, such as the partner ID or third-party ID.</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>File name pattern</p></td><td><p>Filename pattern required by your partner.</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Archival Path</p></td><td><div><div><p>Directory path on the SFTP server at which to store a copy of the file after processing.</p></div><div><div data-type="NOTE">The runtime application attempts to create the archive file up to three times. If permission or connection errors prevent Partner Manager from deleting the file, Partner Manager logs the issue in the runtime application and skips the archival step to prevent duplicate message processing.</div></div></div></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

## See Also

- [Endpoints](endpoints)
- [Creating Endpoints](create-endpoint)
- [Managing Partner and Third-Party Connection Certificates](manage-partner-certificates)
---

# SFTP Send Endpoint Settings

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

SFTP send endpoint settings configure where to send B2B messages transmitted over SFTP. There are
two types of SFTP send endpoints:

- Send to Partners
  - Target endpoint in an outbound message flow through which transformed B2B messages are sent to
    partners, either directly or through third-party connections
  - Acknowledgement endpoint in an inbound message flow

  A host, partner, or third-party connection can own SFTP Send to Partners endpoints.

- Target at Host

  Target endpoint in an inbound message flow through which transformed JSON and XML messages are
  sent to the backend applications.

  Only a host can own SFTP Target at Host endpoints.

Create SFTP send endpoints by configuring these settings on the **<host>** or **<partner>** pages.
You can also create these endpoints when you configure an outbound message flow:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Owner</p></td><td><p>Host, partner, or third-party connection that owns the endpoint.</p></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Usage</p></td><td><p>Endpoint function (<code>Send to partners</code> or <code>Target at host</code>).</p></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Protocol</p></td><td><p>Protocol that the endpoint supports (<code>SFTP</code>).</p></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Endpoint Name</p></td><td><p>Automatically generated name used to identify the endpoint, which you can modify manually.</p></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Description</p></td><td><p>User-supplied value that describes the purpose of the endpoint.</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Retry interval (in seconds)</p></td><td><p>Frequency of reconnection attempts when unable to connect to the remote AS2 service. The default is <code>30 Seconds</code>.</p></td><td><p>No</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Maximum retry attempts</p></td><td><p>Maximum number of reconnection attempts to make. If you donât want Partner Manager to retry after an initial connection failure, set this value to <code>0</code>.</p></td><td><p>No</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Host</p></td><td><p>SFTP server host, such as <code>sftp.mulesoft.com</code>, <code>localhost</code>, or <code>192.168.0.1</code>. The host must be accessible from your Mule environment.</p></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Port</p></td><td><p>Port number of the SFTP.</p></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Authentication type</p></td><td><div><div><p>Authentication method used to secure the SFTP endpoint. Values are:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><strong>None</strong></p><div><p>Provides no authentication for the endpoint</p></div></li><li><p><strong>Basic</strong></p><div><p>Authenticates by using a username and password</p></div></li><li><p><strong>Identity Key</strong></p><div><p>Authenticates by using a private SSH identity key</p></div></li><li><p><strong>Basic and Identity Key</strong></p><div><p>Authenticates by using a username, password, and public SSH identity key</p></div></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Username</p></td><td><p>(Applies to all authentication types except <code>None</code>)</p><p>Username for the SFTP server.</p></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Password</p></td><td><p>(Applies to these authentication types: <code>Basic</code>, <code>Basic and Identity Key</code>)</p><p>Password for the SFTP server.</p></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>SSH</p></td><td><div><div><p>(Applies to these authentication types: <code>Identity Key</code>, <code>Basic and Identity Key</code>)</p></div><div><p>SSH private key file. The following considerations apply:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Send and receive endpoints that have the same owner can use the same SSH private key file.</p></li><li><p>Multiple message flows for the same or different partners can reference endpoints that use the same SSH private key file.</p></li></ul></div><div><p>You can either select a private key file (<code>.pem</code>) thatâs already configured in Anypoint Partner Manager or create a new configuration for a private key file.</p></div><div><p>To create a new configuration for a private key file:</p></div><div><ol><li><p>In the <strong>&lt;partner&gt; SSH</strong> or <strong>&lt;host&gt; SSH</strong> field, click <strong>Select</strong>.</p></li><li><p>In the <strong>Select a &lt;partner&gt; ssh</strong> or <strong>Select a &lt;host&gt; ssh</strong> dialog, click <strong>New</strong>.</p></li><li><p>Complete the fields in the <strong>New certificate for &lt;partner&gt;</strong> or <strong>New certificate for &lt;host&gt;</strong> dialog:</p><div><ul><li><p><strong>Identity Key name</strong></p><div><p>Enter the name of the private key file.</p></div></li><li><p><strong>&lt;partner&gt; identity key PEM file</strong> or <strong>&lt;host&gt; identity key PEM file</strong></p><div><p>Click <strong>Choose file</strong> to select the private key file.</p></div></li><li><p><strong>Key passphrase</strong></p><div><p>Enter the passphrase for the private key file.</p></div></li><li><p><strong>Expiration date</strong></p><div><p>Enter the date on which you want the private key file to expire. This date is a reminder for you, but Anypoint Partner Manager doesnât enforce it.</p></div></li></ul></div></li><li><p>Click <strong>Create</strong>.</p></li></ol></div></div></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Target Path</p></td><td><p>Path on your SFTP server to EDI files that were generated from outbound message flows. This is also the path on your SFTP server for storing transformed JSON and XML files generated from inbound message flows.</p></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Filename pattern</p></td><td><p>Filename pattern required by your partner or back-end application.</p></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

## See Also

- [Endpoints](endpoints)
- [Creating Endpoints](create-endpoint)
- [Filename Patterns for Send Endpoints](file-name-pattern)
---

# Endpoints

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Endpoints define the transport protocol configurations necessary to receive or send messages with
external partners or internal backend applications.

Anypoint Partner Manager uses endpoints to:

- Receive messages from partners
- Receive messages from backend applications
- Define transport protocols
- Specify message or message acknowledgment destinations

## Endpoint Ownership

Depending on the endpoint type, you can assign the host, partner, or third-party connection as the
endpoint owner to control the use of endpoints within inbound and outbound message flows.

When creating partner-facing endpoints directly from a partner or third-party connection, the
ownership of the endpoint is automatically assigned to the entity from which the endpoint was
created.

In a message flow, endpoint availability depends on the endpoint ownership:

- If an endpoint is owned by the host, any partner or third-party connection can select the
  endpoint.
- If an endpoint is owned by a specific partner or third-party connection, then that endpoint is
  available only to the partner or third-party connection to which the endpoint is assigned.

## Supported Endpoint Protocols

The configuration of Partner Manager endpoints depends on the endpoint type and protocol. The
following table describes the endpoint types, along with their supported protocols and possible
owners:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Endpoint Type</th><th>Protocol</th><th>Owner</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Source at Host</p></td><td><p>FTP, HTTP, HTTPS, SFTP</p></td><td><p>Host</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Target at Host</p></td><td><p>FTP, HTTP, HTTPS, SFTP</p></td><td><p>Host</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Receive from Partners</p></td><td><p>AS2</p></td><td><p>Host</p></td></tr><tr><td></td><td><p>FTP, HTTP, HTTPS, SFTP</p></td><td><p>Host, partner, third-party connection</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Send to Partners</p></td><td><p>AS2</p></td><td><p>Partner, third-party connection</p></td></tr><tr><td></td><td><p>FTP, HTTP, HTTPS, SFTP</p></td><td><p>Host, partner, third-party connection</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Endpoint Runtime Services

Using dynamic configurations, Partner Manager deploys generic endpoint services that can receive
data from or send data to different endpoint configurations. You can reuse the endpoint
configurations of all supported protocols across various message flows and partners.

Partner Managerâs deployment orchestration service inspects the message flow and associated
configuration objects and determines which endpoint services to deploy or update based on whether
any new endpoint configurations are added, updated, or removed.

The number and type of endpoint services deployed to your runtime depend on the transport protocols
used to exchange data with your partners and backend systems.

Each unique AS2, HTTP, and HTTPS receive endpoint configuration results in the deployment of its own
runtime application. You can view the runtime application name corresponding to a given
configuration in Partner Manager, from the endpoint details page.

The endpoints for the transport protocols are always managed within a single generic endpoint
service for the protocol and direction. The following table shows the prefix for the endpoint
services:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Protocol</th><th>Direction</th><th>Application Name Prefix</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>AS2</p></td><td><p>Receive</p></td><td><p>b2b-inbound-as2-</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>AS2</p></td><td><p>Send</p></td><td><p>b2b-outbound-as2-</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>FTP</p></td><td><p>Receive</p></td><td><p>b2b-inbound-ftp-</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>FTP</p></td><td><p>Send</p></td><td><p>b2b-outbound-ftp-</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>HTTP or HTTPS</p></td><td><p>Receive</p></td><td><p>b2b-inbound-http-</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>HTTP or HTTPS</p></td><td><p>Send</p></td><td><p>b2b-outbound-http-</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>SFTP</p></td><td><p>Receive</p></td><td><p>b2b-inbound-sftp-</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>SFTP</p></td><td><p>Send</p></td><td><p>b2b-outbound-sftp-</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

If you deploy to CloudHub, you can assign static IP addresses to the endpoint applications that
connect to external systems from Runtime Manager, if your organization or your partners require
allowlisting IP addresses. You can share the
[static IP](../../cloudhub/managing-applications-on-cloudhub#static-ips) address assigned to your
endpoint application with your partners for them to allowlist the IP address, allowing the
application to connect with their server.

## See Also

- [Creating Endpoints](create-endpoint)
- [Deploying AS2, HTTP, and HTTPS Receive Endpoints](deploying-receive-endpoints)
---

# Filename Patterns for Send Endpoints

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Using property placeholders, Partner Manager enables you to configure a template that specifies
properties and their order in the filename patterns for files delivered to SFTP and AS2 send
endpoints.

When filename pattern fields are configured with property placeholders (specified within `${ }`),
Partner Manager dynamically populates the filename with updated information at runtime. Any filename
property field that is not specified within the placeholder is static and is not updated at runtime.

The following table shows the property placeholders that Partner Manager supports and the dynamic
value the placeholder is replaced with at runtime. All dates and times must be in UTC format.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Placeholder</th><th>Replaced with</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>${DocType}</p></td><td><p>EDI Document Type</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>${GSSender}</p></td><td><p>Group ID of the Sender</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>${GSReceiver}</p></td><td><p>Group ID of the Receiver</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>${ISASender}</p></td><td><p>Interchange ID of the Sender</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>${ISAReceiver}</p></td><td><p>Interchange ID of the Receiver</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>${uuid}</p></td><td><p>Unique Identifier: for example: <code>93de0793-63e1-5a06-aedf-fcd052ed2155</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p>${YYYY}</p></td><td><p>Year: for example, <code>2021</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p>${MM}</p></td><td><p>Month: for example, <code>04</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p>${DD}</p></td><td><p>Date: for example, <code>14</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p>${HH}</p></td><td><p>Hour: for example, <code>15</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p>${mm}</p></td><td><p>Minute: for example, <code>25</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p>${SS}</p></td><td><p>Seconds: for example, <code>46</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p>${sss}</p></td><td><p>Milliseconds: for example, <code>345</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p>${timeInMillis}</p></td><td><p>Current time in milliseconds: for example, <code>1648805073834</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p>${ICN}</p></td><td><p>Interchange Control Number</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>${GCN}</p></td><td><p>Group Control Number</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>${BusKey}</p></td><td><p>Primary Business Key value: for example, <code>PO12345</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p>${FormatTypeExt}</p></td><td><div><div><p>File format:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>edifact</code></p></li><li><p><code>json</code></p></li><li><p><code>x12</code></p></li><li><p><code>xml</code></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Examples

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Filename Pattern</th><th>Generated Filename</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>${DocType}_${ISASender}_${YYYYMMDD}_${HHmmSSsss}_${ICN}.edi</p></td><td><p><code>850_NTORETAIL_20201102_192655521_300001.edi</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p>INVOICE_${SenderGSID}_${ICN}.${FormatTypeExt}</p></td><td><p><code>INVOICE_MYTHICALSUPPLIERS_12355343.xml</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p>${uuid}.edi</p></td><td><p><code>93de0793-63e1-5a06-aedf-fcd052ed2155.edi</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p>${DocType}-${YYYY}-${MM}-${DD}-${HHmmSSsss}.dat</p></td><td><p><code>204-2020-06-24-192655521.dat</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p>${DocType}_${ISASender}_${YYYYMMDD}_${HHmmSSsss}_${ICN}.${FormatTypeExt}</p></td><td><p><code>850_NTORETAIL_20201102_192655521_300001.json</code></p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## See Also

- [AS2 Send Endpoints](endpoint-as2-send)
- [SFTP Send Endpoints](endpoint-sftp-send)
---

# Getting Started with Partner Manager

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Partner Manager provides a simplified, configuration-driven B2B integration solution to manage B2B
partner ecosystems. To use Partner Manager, follow these steps:

1.  [Assign permissions to Partner Manager users](#assign-permissions).
2.  [Configure Partner Manager deployment settings](#deployment-settings).
3.  [Set up the Payload Storage API](#payload-storage-api).
4.  [Configure a host profile](#configure-host-profile).
5.  [Configure a profile for each of your partners](#configure-partners).
6.  [Optionally configure third-party connections](#configure-third-party).
7.  [Configure message flows](#configure-message-flows).
8.  [Deploy and test configured message flows](#deploy-test-message-flows).
9.  [Monitor transmissions and individual messages](#monitor-transmissions).
10. [Troubleshoot rejected or overdue acknowledgements](#troubleshoot-acks).

## Assign Permissions to Partner Manager Users

For each environment, use access management to assign permissions to each Partner Manager user. You
can assign the following permissions:

- **Partner Manager Administrator**
  - Has full permissions

- **View Host, Partners, and Message Flows**
  - Has view-only access to the host, partner, and message flow configurations
  - Canât view transaction activity

- **Manage Partners and Message Flows**
  - Can create, modify, and delete partners or message flow configurations
  - Can view partner configurations
  - Canât view and manage transaction activity

- **Manage Activity**
  - Can view and manage transaction activity
  - Canât view or modify either partner or message flow configurations

- **Manage Host**
  - Can create, modify, and delete host configurations
  - Canât view or modify partner configurations or transaction activity

  This access applies even if the user has the Anypoint Platform Org Admin role.

- **View Activity**
  - Has view-only access to transaction activity
  - Canât view or modify either partner or message flow configurations

For more information, see
[Permissions Available in Anypoint Platform](../../access-management/permissions-by-product).

## Configure Partner Manager Deployment Settings

You can deploy Partner Manager runtime applications to CloudHub 1.0, CloudHub 2.0, or Anypoint
Runtime Fabric. To configure deployment options, use the **Partner Manager Settings** page in access
management.

For more information, see [Deploying Partner Manager Runtime Apps](deploy-partner-manager).

## Set Up the Payload Storage API

Partner Manager requires that you deploy a payload storage API for storing transmission information.
You can connect this API to your choice of a storage location, such as a database, Amazon S3, or a
file system.

For more information, see [Configuring a Payload Storage API](setup-payload-storage-api).

## Configure a Host Profile

Use the **<host>** page in Partner Manager to configure a host profile for your Anypoint Platform
organization or business group. A host profile consists of the EDI and AS2 identifiers used by your
organization, associated digital certificates and keystores, host-owned endpoints, message types,
Payload Storage API details, and global settings for EDIFACT receive messages.

For more information, see [Configuring and Managing a Host Profile](configure-host).

## Configure a Partner Profile for Each of Your Partners

A partner profile consists of partner-specific settings such as:

- The partnerâs name
- Identifiers (such as the AS2 ID, X12-ISA, X12 GS, and so on)
- Digital certificates (such as X.509 certificates)
- Endpoints to use for sending and receiving messages to and from your partner
- The acknowledgments, validations, and control numbers for each message format that the partner
  uses

Configure a partner profile for each of your partners. If you plan to use only host endpoints, you
donât need to create partner-specific endpoints.

For more information, see [Configuring and Managing Partners](configure-partner).

## Configure Third-Party Connections

If your partners use third-party service providers such as VANs or 3PLs (third-party logistics
providers) that manage the B2B connectivity with your organization on behalf of your partners, then
create configurations for these connections.

Creating third-party connections is similar to configuring partner profiles, except that the
acknowledgements, validations, and control numbers associated with message formats are configured
only at the partner level.

For more information, see [Configuring and Managing Third-Party Connections](configure-third-party).

## Configure Message Flows

Message flows contain the following components, which execute end-to-end B2B message processing:

- Partner identifiers
- Receive and send endpoints
- Source and target message types
- DataWeave translation maps

Create inbound message flows to receive messages from your partners, transform the messages into
your internal application format, and send the transformed message to your backend system.

Create outbound message flows to receive messages from your backend applications, transform the
messages to your partnerâs message format, and send the messages to your partner.

For more information, see [Configuring and Managing Message Flows](message-flows) and
[Message Flow Components](partner-manager-configuration-objects).

## Deploy and Test Configured Message Flows

Deploy and test the message flows in a sandbox environment. After you verify that a message flow is
successful, you can undeploy it from the sandbox and redeploy it to your production environment.

For more information, see [Deploying and Testing Message Flows](deploy-message-flows) and
[Undeploying Message Flows](undeploy-message-flows).

## Monitor Transmissions and Individual Messages

Monitor transmission activity by searching for transactions based on criteria such as the:

- Partner name
- Message direction
- Send and receive dates
- Messaging processing status
- Partner and host message type

You can also search for transactions based on any custom message attributes that you defined for the
message type using DataWeave.

Monitor individual message activity by searching for messages based on criteria such as the:

- Partner name
- Message direction
- Date range within which the message was sent
- Acknowledgement status
- Messaging process status
- Partner and host message types

You can also search for individual messages based on any custom message attribute that you defined
for the message type using DataWeave.

For more information, see [Tracking Transmissions](activity-tracking),
[Using Custom Message Attributes with Message Types](use-custom-attributes), and
[Tracking Individual Messages](activity-message-tracking).

## Troubleshoot Rejected or Overdue Acknowledgments

When you monitor transmissions, you can search by acknowledgment status (**Ack Status** field) to
find outbound transmissions that either received an X12 997 or EDIFACT CONTRL rejection or that are
overdue for the functional acknowledgment. Then, you can review the X12 997 or EDIFACT CONTRL
payload received from your partners and add a comment to indicate the action taken.

For more information, see [EDI Acknowledgment Reconciliation](edi-ack-reconciliation).

## See Also

- [Partner Manager Overview](./)
- [Partner Manager Deployment Architecture](partner-manager-architecture)
- [Setting up the Partner Manager Environment](setup)
---

# Inbound Message Flows

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

An inbound message flow does the following:

1.  Receives application messages from your partner, either directly or through a third-party
    connection
2.  Transforms the messages to the format used by your back-end applications
3.  Forwards the messages to those applications

The following diagram shows the components of an inbound message flow:

![A flowchart showing an inbound message flow](_images/pm-inbound-message-flow.png)

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Callout</th><th>Setting</th><th>Description</th><th>Supported Formats or Protocols</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><span><img src="_images/pm-annotation-1.png" alt="A circle with a number 1 inside"></span></p></td><td><p>Receiving endpoint</p></td><td><p>Endpoint at which a host receives transmissions from a partner</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p>AS2</p></li><li><p>FTP</p></li><li><p>HTTP</p></li><li><p>HTTPS</p></li><li><p>SFTP</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><span><img src="_images/pm-annotation-2.png" alt="A circle with a number 2 inside"></span></p></td><td><p>Source message type</p></td><td><p>Message type of the received message payload</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p>CSV</p></li><li><p>EDIFACT</p></li><li><p>JSON</p></li><li><p>X12</p></li><li><p>XML</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><span><img src="_images/pm-annotation-3.png" alt="A circle with a number 3 inside"></span></p></td><td><p>Map</p></td><td><p>DataWeave map used to transform the source message type to the target message type</p></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><span><img src="_images/pm-annotation-4.png" alt="A circle with a number 4 inside"></span></p></td><td><p>Target message type</p></td><td><p>Format into which the DataWeave map transforms received messages</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p>JSON</p></li><li><p>XML</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><span><img src="_images/pm-annotation-5.png" alt="A circle with a number 5 inside"></span></p></td><td><p>Target endpoint</p></td><td><p>Endpoint to which the transformed message is sent (typically, a process API)</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p>FTP</p></li><li><p>HTTP</p></li><li><p>HTTPS</p></li><li><p>SFTP</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

## See Also

- [Creating Inbound Message Flows](create-inbound-message-flow)
- [Deploying and Testing Message Flows](deploy-message-flows)
- [Modifying Message Flow Settings](manage-message-flows)
- [Inbound Message Routing](inbound-message-routing)
---

# Inbound Message Routing

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Depending on the endpoint type and protocol, the successful routing of your partnerâs send messages
to the configured source endpoints uses the message type ID, file-naming pattern, and content type.

## Messages That Use an AS2 Receive from Partners Endpoint

To successfully route inbound messages that use an AS2 Receive from Partners endpoint, your partner
does the following:

- For non-EDI files (JSON or XML), uses a filename that begins with `{message-type-id}_`.

  For example, if the message type is named `custom-po`, the filename must begin with `custom-po_`.

  There are no filename restrictions for EDI files.

- Sets the AS2 MIME-Type in the inbound AS2 message as follows, depending on the message type:
  - EDIFACT: `application/EDIFACT` or `text/plain`
  - JSON: `application/json`
  - X12: `application/EDI-X12` or `text/plain`
  - XML: `application/xml`
  - CSV: `text/csv`

## Messages That Use an FTP or SFTP Endpoint

The process that Partner Manager uses to route inbound messages differs depending on whether the
messages are EDIFACT or X12 EDI messages or non-EDI messages, such as CSV, JSON, or XML.

### EDI Messages

Partner Manager routes an inbound EDIFACT or X12 EDI message based on the EDI identifier, such as
ISA identifier for X12 or UNB identifier for EDIFACT, in the message content.

### Non-EDI Messages

Partner Manager routes an inbound non-EDI message, such as CSV, JSON, or XML, based on either the
associated message type or the filename:

- If you specified an associated message type in the endpoint configuration, Partner Manager routes
  the message based on that message type.
- If you didnât specify an associated message type in the endpoint configuration, Partner Manager
  routes the message based on the filename. The filename must begin with `{message-type-id}_` and
  have an appropriate file extension:
  - `.json` for JSON files
  - `.xml` for XML files
  - `*.csv` for CSV files

For example, if the message type is named `custom-po` and itâs formatted as XML, the filename must
begin with `custom-po_` and end with `.xml`.

### Receive from Partners Endpoint

When Partner Manager deploys endpoint applications in Mule runtime, the protocol and endpoint
configurations dictate the steps the applications follow, as shown in the diagram. Next, the
document processing service routes the message via the `dps-inbound-queue`. The API replication
service receives a copy of the message via the `tracking-queue`, stores the message payload in your
storage, and updates the tracking information in Partner Manager.

[![A flowchart of the inbound message processing for AS2, HTTP, and SFTP/FTP protocols](_images/pm-inbound-message-routing2.png)](_images/pm-inbound-message-routing2.png)

### Document Processing

The document processing application uses the inbound messageâs attributes, such as content-type
header, URL relative path, file name, and payload, to route the message, as shown in the diagram. If
Partner Manager doesnât find a matching message flow, the transmission receives a **Failed** status
and an error message shows.

![A flowchart that depicts message flow processing and matching](_images/pm-dps-inbound-processing2.png)

## Messages That Use an HTTP or HTTPS Receive from Partners Endpoint

To successfully route inbound messages that use an HTTP Receive from Partners endpoint or an HTTPS
Receive from Partners endpoint, your partner does the following:

- For EDI messages (EDIFACT or X12), posts them to the base URL and sets the Content-Type value in
  the incoming HTTP request as follows, depending on the message format:
  - EDIFACT: `application/EDIFACT` or `text/plain`
  - JSON: `application/json`
  - X12: `application/EDI-X12` or `text/plain`
  - XML: `application/xml`
  - CSV: `text/csv`

- For non-EDI messages (JSON or XML), includes the message type ID in the relative path of the HTTP
  request.

  For example, suppose you have the following scenario in a CloudHub deployment:
  - The base URL of the HTTP or HTTPS Receive from Partners endpoint is
    `https://b2b-inbound-http-abcd.us-e2.cloudhub.io:443/receive1/`.
  - The expected message type ID for the inbound message is `custom-po`.

    In this case, your partner should post the message to the
    `https://b2b-inbound-http-abcd.us-e2.cloudhub.io:443/receive1/custom-po` URL.

## See Also

- [Inbound Message Flows](inbound-message-flows)
- [Create Inbound Message Flows](create-inbound-message-flow)
- [Track Transmission Activity](activity-tracking)
---

# Anypoint Partner Manager for B2B Integration

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Perform bidirectional Business-to-Business (B2B) message exchanges with your external business
partner ecosystem. Anypoint Partner Manager supports multiple transport protocols, including AS2,
HTTP, HTTPS, SFTP, and FTP, and handles various message formats such as CSV, EDIFACT, EDI X12, JSON,
and XML. Partner Manager enables use cases such as order to cash, procure to pay, logistics
planning, warehouse management, and transportation execution.

## Partner Manager Capabilities

Use Partner Manager to:

- Perform technical onboarding of your partners.
- Set up, manage, and monitor business message flows between your systems and your partners'
  systems.
- Track and report on business transactions to achieve greater operational efficiency in B2B
  transactions.
- View meta information, validation errors, and all messages included with each transmission
  payload.

  Transmission payloads can contain one or more business messages.

Watch the Partner Manager Product Spotlight video to see a quick overview of Partner Manager:

![](https://play.vidyard.com/Byix7eAHCZyWCnt7tkFNGo.jpg)

## Before You Begin Using Partner Manager

Before you begin using Partner Manager, you must have:

- An Anypoint Platform account with the Partner Manager entitlement.
- AS2, EDIFACT, and X12 Connector entitlements, if your organization uses these standards and
  protocols.
- One or more of the user permissions for Partner Manager.
- The Partner Manager Administrator role, which can assign permission levels to all users who need
  access to B2B message flows in their sandbox or production environment.
- The required Mule runtime capacity, either vCore or messages and flows, to manage your B2B
  transaction workload needs.
- Storage for the B2B transmission payloads you send or receive, such as a database, Amazon S3, and
  Azure Blob Storage.
- Familiarity with EDI message formats.
- Anypoint Studio 7.4 or later.
- The ability to create DataWeave maps to use for performing data transformations.

## Partner Manager Concepts

To use Partner Manager, you must understand the following concepts:

### Host

Your Anypoint Platform organization or business group. A Partner Manager implementation has a single
host for which you define a host profile.

### Partners

The partners with whom you send and receive B2B transmissions. You define a partner profile for each
partner in your B2B ecosystem.

### Payload Storage API

An API that provides Partner Manager with a standardized way to connect to your solution for storing
transmission content.

### Message Flows

The paths along which a B2B message travels from its source, through a DataWeave translation map, to
its target. There are two types of message flows:

- Inbound Message Flows

  Inbound message flows receive messages from partners, including messages that go through
  third-party connections. When you create an inbound message flow, you can validate and transform
  the inbound messages and then send them to your backend applications.

- Outbound message flows

  Outbound message flows receive messages from your backend applications, validate and transform
  these messages into your partnerâs expected format, and send them to your partners directly or
  through third-party connections.

  Message flows consist of [endpoints](#endpoints), [message types](#message-types),
  [sender and receiver identifiers](#identifiers), and [translation maps](#translation-maps).

### Endpoints

Define the transport protocol configurations necessary for:

- Receiving messages from external partners or internal backend applications
- Sending messages to external partners or backend applications

You can configure the following types of endpoints:

- Receive from Partners

  Source endpoint in an inbound message flow that receives inbound B2B messages from partners either
  directly or via third-party connections

- Target at Host

  Target endpoint in an inbound message flow through which transformed messages are sent to the
  backend applications

- Source at Host

  Source endpoint in an outbound message flow that receives application messages from the backend
  applications

- Send to Partners

  Target endpoint in an outbound message flow through which transformed B2B messages are sent to the
  partners either directly or via third-party connections

### Message Types

Define the structure of the transactions that Partner Manager receives or sends. Partner Manager
supports the following message types:

- Receive from Partners

  Defines the structure of the transactions that partners or third-party connections send to the
  host through inbound message flows. You can define [custom message attributes](#custom-attributes)
  for this message type.

- Target at Host

  Defines the structure of the transactions that the host sends to the backend through inbound
  message flows.

- Source at Host

  Defines the structure of the transactions that the backend sends to the host through outbound
  message flows. You can define custom message attributes for this message type. You can define
  [custom message attributes](#custom-attributes) for this message type.

- Send to Partners

  Defines the structure of the transactions that the host sends to partners or third-party
  connections through outbound message flows.

### Sender and Receiver Identifiers

Identifiers in AS2, EDIFACT, and X12 transactions that identify the message senders and receivers.

For X12 messages, the identifiers in the ISA and GS headers provide information that identifies the
appropriate flow to process the transaction.

For EDIFACT messages, the identifiers in the UNB segment provide information that identifies the
appropriate flow to process the transaction.

For other formats, such as CSV and JSON, Partner Manager extracts the identifiers from data payloads
or a message contact by dynamically executing the reference mapping supplied with the message type
definition.

### Custom Message Attributes

User-defined attributes associated with Receive from Partners or Source at Host message types
through reference mapping. Messages extract key data fields, like Order Number, Invoice Number, and
Shipment ID, as business transactions are processed. After a transaction processes, users can find
the message attribute label and value by searching transactions on the Activity page in Partner
Manager or via API calls.

### Translation Maps

DataWeave maps transform business transactions between your partnerâs message formats and your
organizationâs enterprise application message formats. You create the maps in Studio and import them
into Partner Manager when you configure message flows.

### Templates

Prebuilt templates that convert your B2B message flow configurations into runtime applications. When
MuleSoft upgrades a template, you can upgrade your message flows to use them to take advantage of
new capabilities that enable better integration with your partnerâs ecosystem.

## Next Step

Continue to [Getting Started with Partner Manager](get-started).

## See Also

- [Partner Manager Architecture](partner-manager-architecture)
- [Anypoint Partner Manager Setup](setup)
---

# Managing AS2 Host Keystores

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

AS2 host keystores consist of a public certificate and private key. Configure at least one AS2 host
keystore if you use AS2 endpoints.

## Create an AS2 Host Keystore

To create an AS2 host keystore:

1.  In the sidebar, access the **<host>** page.
2.  In the **Certificates** section, click **New**.
3.  In the **Certificate Type** field, select **Private Keypair**.
4.  In the **Keystore Name** field, enter the name of the keystore file to store your organizationâs
    private key.
5.  In the **<host> Public Certificate** field, click **Choose file** to select a public
    certificate.
6.  In the **<host> Private Key File** field, click **Choose file** to select a key file.
7.  In the **Key Passphrase** field, enter a passphrase for protecting the private key.

## Update an AS2 Host Keystore

When the certificate for your AS2 keystore is about to expire, perform the following steps to obtain
a new one. You can update the keystore in a non-production environment and coordinate when to make
the change in production with your partners.

1.  Depending on your organizationâs policies, create a self-signed certificate and key pair, or
    obtain a new AS2 certificate from your certificate authority.
2.  Define a date on which you plan to switch to the new certificate.
3.  Share the new public certificate with your AS2 partners to give them sufficient lead time to
    plan the certificate switch on their side.

When itâs time to update the keystore with the new certificate, follow these steps:

1.  In the sidebar, access the **<host>** page.
2.  In the **Certificates** section, click the keystore to update and then click **Edit**.
3.  In the **<host> public certificate** field, click **Choose file**, navigate to the updated
    public certificate, and click **Open**.
4.  In the **<host> private key file** field, click **Choose file**, navigate to the private key
    file, and click **Open**.
5.  In the **Key passphrase** field, enter the passphrase for the new keystore.
6.  Click **Update keystore**.
7.  After the keystore updates, do either of the following:
    - If you have only one AS2 Receive from Partners or Send to Partners endpoint that uses the
      keystore, open one message flow that references the endpoint and redeploy the flow.
    - If you have more than one AS2 Receive from Partners or Send to Partners endpoint that uses the
      keystore, redeploy one message flow for each endpoint that uses the keystore.

    Anypoint Partner Manager updates the inbound and outbound AS2 applications in the runtime. The
    update can take up to 10 minutes to complete.

## Remove an AS2 Host Keystore

You can remove an AS2 host keystore from Partner Manager if it isnât associated with an endpoint.
You cannot delete a host keystore if it is the only keystore that is uploaded to Partner Manager.

To remove an AS2 host keystore:

1.  In the sidebar, access the **<host>** page.
2.  In the **Certificates** section, click the keystore certificate to remove.
3.  Click **Delete**.

## See Also

- [Certificate and Keystore Storage](certificates)
- [AS2 Receive Endpoint Settings](endpoint-as2-receive)
- [AS2 Send Endpoint Settings](endpoint-as2-send)
- [Managing Host Certificates](manage-host-certificates)
---

# Managing Host Certificates

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Anypoint Partner Manager supports the following types of host certificates:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Certificate Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Used by</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Identity key</p></td><td><div><div><p>Private key used for authentication</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The following types of endpoints, when the authentication type is <code>Identity Key</code> or <code>Basic and Identity Key</code>:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>SFTP Receive from Partners endpoints owned by the host</p></li><li><p>SFTP Source at Host endpoints</p></li><li><p>SFTP Send to Partners endpoints owned by the host</p></li><li><p>SFTP Target at Host endpoints</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Public certificate</p></td><td><p>Certificate generated by a certificate authority (CA)</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p>HTTPS Send to Partners endpoints owned by the host, when the TLS context is set to <code>Use a self-signed certificate</code></p></li><li><p>HTTPS Target at Host endpoints, when the TLS context is set to <code>Use a self-signed certificate</code></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

> [!NOTE] For AS2 endpoints, you must also configure at least one host keystore, as described in
> [Managing AS2 Host Keystores](manage-as2-host-keystores).

## Add a Host Certificate

To add a host certificate:

1.  In the sidebar, access the **<host>** page.
2.  In the **Certificates** section, click **New**.
3.  Select a certificate type.
4.  Enter the settings for the selected certificate type:
    - [Public Certificate](#public-certificate)
    - [Identity Key](#identity-key)

5.  Click **Save**.

### Public Certificate Settings

To configure settings for a public certificate:

1.  In the **Certificate name** field, enter the name of the public certificate.
2.  In the **Certificate** field, click **Choose file** and select a public certificate.

### Identity Key Certificate Settings

To configure settings for an identity key certificate:

1.  In the **Identity Key Name** field, enter the name of the identity key certificate.
2.  In the **<host> Identity Key PEM File** field, click **Choose file** and select an identity key
    certificate.
3.  In the **Key Passphrase** field, enter a passphrase for protecting the identity key certificate.
4.  In the **Expiration Date** field, optionally enter an expiration date for the certificate. This
    date is a reminder for you, but Anypoint Partner Manager doesnât enforce it.

## Update a Host Certificate

Update a host certificate by selecting a different certificate file to associate with the it:

1.  In the sidebar, access the **<host>** page.
2.  In the **Certificates** section, click the name of the certificate that you want to update.
3.  Click **Edit**.
4.  Click **Choose File** and select a certificate file.
5.  Click **Update**.

## Remove a Host Certificate

If a host certificate isnât associated with an endpoint, you can remove it from Anypoint Partner
Manager:

1.  In the sidebar, access the **<host>** page.
2.  In the **Certificates** section, hover over the name of the host certificate to remove.
3.  Click the trash can icon on the right.

## Enforce Certificate Expiration Dates for AS2 Requests

To prevent inbound AS2 requests with expired certificates from being processed by Anypoint Partner
Manager:

1.  In the sidebar, access the **<host>** page.
2.  In the **Global endpoint settings** section, click **AS2** in the **Inbound endpoint settings**
    column.
3.  Select **Enforce AS2 certificate expiration date**.
4.  Click **Save**.

## See Also

- [Certificate and Keystore Storage](certificates)
- [Configuring and Managing a Host Profile](configure-host)
- [Managing Partner and Third-Party Connection Certificates](manage-partner-certificates)
---

# Modifying Message Flow Settings

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Active message flows between you and your partners donât require much maintenance. However, you
might need to:

- Change flow identifiers, endpoints, or maps.
- Switch from a sandbox environment and reclaim production-environment resources.
- Delete the flow when it becomes inactive, as described in
  [Deleting Message Flows](delete-message-flows).

> [!IMPORTANT] Before making changes, carefully consider the impact on the message flow.

## Modify the Settings of an Inbound Message Flow

To modify the settings of an inbound message flow:

1.  In the sidebar, select **Message Flows**.
2.  On the **Message flows** page, select the message flow to modify.
3.  Change any of the following settings:

    <table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th><strong>Setting</strong></th><th><strong>Actions</strong></th><th><strong>Additional Information</strong></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Receive from &lt;partner&gt;</p></td><td><p>Select different identifiers for the partner.</p></td><td><p><a href="partner-manager-identifiers">Partner Manager Identifiers</a></p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Receiving endpoint &lt;endpoint name&gt;</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p>Select a different receiving endpoint.</p></li><li><p>(AS2 endpoint) Select a different AS2 sender.</p></li><li><p>(AS2 endpoint) View the AS2 certificate associated with the endpoint.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p><a href="create-endpoint">Creating Endpoints</a></p></li><li><p><a href="partner-manager-identifiers">Sender and Receiver Identifiers</a></p></li><li><p><a href="certificates">Certificate Settings</a></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Source message</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p>Select a different receive from partners message type.</p></li><li><p>(EDIFACT or X12 message type) View current validation settings.</p></li><li><p>(EDIFACT or X12 message type) Indicate whether to send a 997 functional acknowledgment and select the endpoint to which to send the acknowledgment.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p><a href="partner-manager-create-message-type">Creating Message Types</a></p></li><li><p><a href="edi-ack-reconciliation">EDI Acknowledgement Reconciliation</a></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Map</p></td><td><p>View the associated translation map, add additional source attributes to the map, or import another one.</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p><a href="partner-manager-maps">Translation Maps</a></p></li><li><p><a href="access-source-message-attributes">Adding Additional Source Attributes to a Translation Map</a></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Target at &lt;host&gt;</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p>Select a different Target at Host message type.</p></li><li><p>Select a different Target at Host endpoint.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p><a href="partner-manager-create-message-type">Creating Message Types</a></p></li><li><p><a href="create-endpoint">Creating Endpoints</a></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

4.  Verify that any modified sections of the flow show a green checkmark next to them and then click
    **Deploy** to deploy the updated message flow to Mule.

    > [!NOTE] It can take five minutes or so for your configuration changes to sync with Mule.

5.  To test your modified message flow, see
    [Deploying and Testing Message Flows](deploy-message-flows).

After you confirm the successful deployment of your message flow, you can track its processed
messages from the **Activity** page.

## Modify the Settings of an Outbound Message Flow

To modify the settings of an outbound message flow:

1.  In the sidebar, select **Message Flows**.
2.  On the **Message flows** page, select **<Message flow name>**.
3.  Change any of the following settings:

    <table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th><strong>Subsection</strong></th><th><strong>Actions</strong></th><th><strong>Additional Information</strong></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Source at &lt;host&gt;</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p>Select a different Source at Host endpoint.</p></li><li><p>Select a different Source at Host message type.</p></li><li><p>Select different reference IDs to use for outbound routing.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p><a href="create-endpoint">Creating Endpoints</a></p></li><li><p><a href="partner-manager-create-message-type">Creating Message Types</a></p></li><li><p><a href="partner-manager-identifiers">Sender and Receiver Identifiers</a></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Map</p></td><td><p>View the associated translation map, add additional source attributes to the map, or import another one.</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p><a href="partner-manager-maps">Translation Maps</a></p></li><li><p><a href="access-source-message-attributes">Adding Additional Source Attributes to a Translation Map</a></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Message type</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p>Select a different Send to Partners message type.</p></li><li><p>(EDIFACT or X12 message type) Indicate whether to send a 997 functional acknowledgment and select the endpoint to which to send the acknowledgment.</p></li><li><p>(EDIFACT or X12 message type) View current validation settings.</p></li><li><p>Select a different identifier for the host and partner. Each identifier field accepts one value and doesnât allow multiple identifiers.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p><a href="partner-manager-create-message-type">Creating Message Types</a></p></li><li><p><a href="edi-ack-reconciliation">EDI Acknowledgment Reconciliation</a></p></li><li><p><a href="partner-manager-identifiers">Sender and Receiver Identifiers</a></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Send to &lt;partner&gt;</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p>Select a different Send to Partners endpoint.</p></li><li><p>(AS2 endpoint) View the AS2 associated with the endpoint.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p><a href="create-endpoint">Creating Endpoints</a></p></li><li><p><a href="certificates">Certificate Settings</a></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

4.  Verify that any modified sections of the flow show a green checkmark next to them and then click
    **Deploy** to deploy the configurations to Mule.

    > [!NOTE] It can take five minutes or so for your configuration changes to sync with Mule.

5.  To test your modified message flow, see
    [Deploying and Testing Message Flows](deploy-message-flows).

After you confirm the successful deployment of your message flow, you can track its processed
messages from the **Activity** page.

## See Also

- [Deploying and Undeploying Message Flows](deploy-message-flows)
- [Troubleshooting Anypoint Partner Manager](troubleshooting)
---

# Managing Partner and Third-Party Connection Certificates

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Anypoint Partner Manager supports the following types of partner and third-party-connection
certificates:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Certificate Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Used by</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Public certificate</p></td><td><p>Certificate generated by a certificate (CA)</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p>AS2 Send to Partners endpoints</p></li><li><p>HTTPS Send to Partners endpoints</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Identity key certificate</p></td><td><div><div><p>Private key used for authentication</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p>SFTP Receive from Partners endpoints, when the authentication type is <code>Identity Key</code> or <code>Basic and Identity Key</code></p></li><li><p>SFTP Send to Partners endpoints, when the authentication type is <code>Identity Key</code> or <code>Basic and Identity Key</code></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Add a Certificate

To add a partner or third-party-connection certificate:

1.  In the sidebar, access the **Partners** page.
2.  Click the partner or third-party connection that you want to create a certificate for.
3.  In the **Certificates** section, click **New**.
4.  Select a certificate type.
5.  Enter the settings for the selected certificate type:
    - [Public Certificate](#public-certificate)
    - [Identity Key](#identity-key)

6.  Click **Save**.

After you create a public certificate, you can assign it to the partner to use with AS2 Send to
Partners endpoints. To do this, hover over the AS2 cell in the row containing the certificate and
click **Assign**.

Assigning a certificate to a partner unassigns the previously-assigned certificate.

> [!NOTE] You can also add certificates when you create endpoints. For more information, see
> [HTTP and HTTPS Send Endpoint Settings](endpoint-https-send),
> [SFTP Receive Endpoint Settings](endpoint-sftp-receive-target), and
> [SFTP Send Endpoint Settings](endpoint-sftp-send).

### Public Certificate Settings

To configure settings for a public certificate:

1.  In the **Certificate name** field, enter the name of the public certificate.
2.  In the **Certificate** field, click **Choose file** and select a public certificate.

### Identity Key Certificate Settings

To configure settings for an identity key certificate:

1.  In the **Identity Key Name** field, enter the name of the identity key certificate.
2.  In the **<partner> Identity Key PEM File** field, click **Choose file** and select an identity
    key certificate.
3.  In the **Key passphrase** field, enter a passphrase for protecting the identity key certificate.
4.  In the **Expiration date** field, optionally enter an expiration date for the certificate. This
    date is a reminder for you, but Anypoint Partner Manager doesnât enforce it.

## Update a Certificate

Update a partner or third-party certificate by selecting a different certificate file to associate
with it:

1.  In the sidebar, access the **Partners** page.
2.  Click the partner or third-party connection whose certificate to update.
3.  In the **Certificates** section, click the name of the certificate to update.
4.  Click **Edit**.
5.  Click **Choose File** and select a certificate file.
6.  Click **Update**.

## Remove a Certificate

You can remove an identity key certificate from Partner Manager if the certificate isnât associated
with an endpoint.

You can remove a public certificate from Partner Manager if the certificate isnât associated with an
endpoint and if it isnât assigned to a partner or third-party connection.

To remove a certificate:

1.  In the sidebar, access the **Partners** page.
2.  Click the partner or third-party connection whose certificate you want to remove.
3.  In the **Certificates** section, hover over the name of the certificate configuration to remove.
4.  Click the trash can icon that appears on the right.

## See Also

- [Certificate and Keystore Storage](certificates)
- [Configuring and Managing Partners](configure-partner)
- [Configuring and Managing Third-Party Connections](configure-third-party)
- [Managing Host Certificates](manage-host-certificates)
---

# Translation Maps

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Translation maps are DataWeave transformation files that enable Anypoint Partner Manager to convert
business transactions between your partnerâs message formats and your organizationâs enterprise
application message formats. These maps are essential for B2B integration, allowing seamless data
exchange between different systems and formats.

## Get Started with Translation Maps

To use translation maps in Partner Manager:

1.  Start by creating or customizing a DataWeave maps in Anypoint Studio.
2.  Import the completed map to the [Maps](maps) page in Partner Manager.
3.  Select and deploy the map in message flows.
4.  Version the map as your business requirements evolve.
5.  Apply updated versions of your translation maps without needing to individually upload the map
    file to each message flow.

## Supported Map Formats and Transformations

Partner Manager supports mapping translations between these formats:

- **Partner formats**

  CSV, EDIFACT, X12, JSON, or XML

- **Enterprise application formats**

  CSV, JSON, or XML

## See Also

- [Creating Translation Maps](partner-manager-maps)
- [Adding and Versioning Maps](maps)
- [Creating Inbound Message Flows](create-inbound-message-flow)
- [Creating Outbound Message Flows](create-outbound-message-flow)
- [DataWeave Quickstart](../../dataweave/latest/dataweave-quickstart)
---

# Adding and Versioning Translation Maps

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Translation maps are used to transform messages between different formats. You can add maps to the
Maps page and version them to apply changes across your deployed message flows.

> [!NOTE] You can apply mapping changes across deployed message flows by setting new versions as
> active without needing to upload the updated file to each message flow and redeploy. See
> [Select the Map for a Partner Manager Message Flow](partner-manager-maps#change-map) for more
> information.

## Add a Map

To add a map:

1.  From the Partner Manager sidebar, select **Maps**.
2.  Click **New Map** and select **Send to partner** or **Receive from partner**.
3.  Enter a unique name for the map and click **Save**. This adds the map to the **Maps** page
    without any configuration.
4.  On the map details page, select the message types. For more information about message types, see
    [Create Inbound Message Flows](create-inbound-message-flow) or
    [Create Outbound Message Flows](create-outbound-message-flow).
5.  In the **Map** section, click **Import** and select the `.dwl` file to import.
6.  Assign a unique version label and optionally add a note.
7.  Enter a description by clicking the pencil icon next to the **Description** field.
8.  Click **Save**.

## Add a New Map Version

Adding a new version of a map is a quick way to apply map changes across your deployed message flows
or runtime applications without needing to redeploy individual message flows. Up to 10 versions of a
map can be stored per mapping configuration.

To import a new version of a map:

1.  From the Partner Manager sidebar, select **Maps**. If you donât have the **Maps** tab, update
    your Partner Manager applications to the latest runtime templates from version 2.25.0 or later.
2.  Click the name of the map to open the details page.
3.  Click **Select** in the **Map** section.
4.  Click **Import new file** in the **Select a Map File** window.
5.  Navigate to the `.dwl` file you want to import and click **Open** to add the new version of the
    map.
6.  To replace the currently active map, select the radio button next to the version that you just
    uploaded.
7.  Click **Set as Active**.
8.  Click **Confirm**.

## Delete a Map

To delete a map:

1.  From the Partner Manager sidebar, select **Maps**.
2.  Click the name of the map to open the details page.
3.  Click **Delete**.

> [!NOTE] Only inactive versions of a map can be deleted. To avoid disruption to existing message
> flows, the current active version canât be deleted.

## View Message Flows for a Map

To view the message flows that use a particular map, click the number in the **Message flows**
column for the map. This opens a message flow details window that shows the message flow name and
deployment status.

## See Also

- [Creating Translation Maps](partner-manager-maps)
---

# Configuring and Managing Message Flows

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

A message flow is the path along which a message travels from its source, through a DataWeave
translation map, to its target. You set up message flows to send and receive messages between your
systems and your partners.

Anypoint Partner Manager message flows consist of objects such as endpoints, message types,
identifiers, and mappings. Partner Managerâs message framework orchestrates the processing of your
business transactions from source to target using the configuration objects shown in the following
diagram:

![A flowchart showing a message flow from the source endpoint to the target endpoint](_images/pm-message-flow.png)

You can create message flows in one of the following contexts:

- Inbound

  An inbound message flow receives messages from your partners, transforms them into your internal
  application message format, and sends the transformed messages to your backend systems.

  For more information, see [Inbound Message Flows](inbound-message-flows).

- Outbound

  An outbound message flow receives messages from your backend applications, transforms the messages
  to your partnerâs message format, and sends the EDI messages to your partner.

  For more information, see [Outbound Message Flows](outbound-message-flows).

## See Also

- [Creating Inbound Message Flows](create-inbound-message-flow)
- [Creating Outbound Message Flows](create-outbound-message-flow)
---

# Receive from Partners Message Type Settings

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

The Receive from Partners message type defines the way in which Anypoint Partner Manager transforms
the inbound transactions sent from your partner to your organization. You can define the CSV,
EDIFACT, JSON, X12, or XML format for the Receive from Partners message type.

## CSV

Use the following settings to define CSV as the format for the Receive from Partners message type:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Setting</th><th>Description</th><th>Default</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Usage</p></td><td><p>Message usage. Allowed value is <code>Receive from Partners</code>.</p></td><td></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Format</p></td><td><p>Format of the message. Allowed value is <code>CSV</code>.</p></td><td></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Separator character</p></td><td><p>Character that separates values in the file.</p></td><td><p>,</p></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Quote character</p></td><td><p>Character used as a quote character in the file.</p></td><td><p>""</p></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Escape character</p></td><td><p>Character used as an escape character in the file.</p></td><td><p>\</p></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Header line</p></td><td><p>When selected, Partner Manager skips the header row when it processes a CSV file.</p></td><td><p>Selected</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Ignore empty line</p></td><td><p>When selected, Partner Manager ignores empty lines when it processes a CSV file.</p></td><td><p>Selected</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Reference identifiers and attributes mapping</p></td><td><div><div><p>Uploaded DataWeave map that extracts the reference identifiers and custom attributes from the payload received from the partner:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>The extracted reference identifier values are used for dynamic routing into the appropriate message flow.</p></li><li><p>The extracted custom attributes provide visibility into the lifecycle of your B2B workflow. For more information, see <a href="use-custom-attributes">Using Custom Messaging Attributes with Message Types</a>.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td></td><td><p>No</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## EDIFACT

Use the following settings to define EDIFACT as the format for the Receive from Partners message
type:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Setting</th><th>Description</th><th>Default</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Usage</p></td><td><p>Message usage. Allowed value is <code>Receive from partners</code>.</p></td><td></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Format</p></td><td><p>Format of the message. Allowed value is <code>EDIFACT</code>.</p></td><td></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Version</p></td><td><p>EDIFACT version, such as <code>d10b</code> or <code>d96a</code>.</p></td><td></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Message type</p></td><td><p>EDIFACT message type, such as <code>ORDERS</code>, <code>DESADV</code>, or <code>INVOIC</code>.</p></td><td></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Reference identifiers and attributes mapping</p></td><td><div><div><p>Uploaded DataWeave map that extracts the reference identifiers and custom attributes from the payload received from the partner:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>The extracted reference identifier values are used for dynamic routing into the appropriate message flow.</p></li><li><p>The extracted custom attributes enable you to have visibility into the lifecycle of your B2B workflow. For more information, see <a href="use-custom-attributes">Using Custom Messaging Attributes with Message Types</a>.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td></td><td><p>No</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Schema</p></td><td><div><div><p>EDI schema for the message type. Options include:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><strong>Use standard EDIFACT-&lt;version&gt;</strong><br>Applies the standard EDIFACT schema for the configured format and version.</p></li><li><p><strong>Customize</strong><br>Enables you to import a customized <a href="../../x12-edi-connector/latest/x12-edi-schema-language-reference">EDI schema language</a> file, if you must make adjustments to the standard EDIFACT schema.</p><div><p>When using the customized EDI schema, you can assign a custom name to the message type definition, for example, <code>EDIFACT-d97a-ORDERS-&lt;PartnerName&gt;</code>.</p></div></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Use standard EDIFACT &lt;version&gt;</p></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## JSON or XML

Use the settings in the table to define JSON or XML as the format for the Receive from Partners
message type.

If you process cXML messages with <DOCTYPE> tags, you must set the `minMuleVersion` to 4.5.0 or
higher and upgrade your runtime environment in Access Management. For more information, see Partner
Manager deployment settings for [CloudHub](cloudhub-deployment-settings),
[CloudHub 2.0](cloudhub2-deployment-settings), and [Runtime Fabric](rtf-deployment-settings).

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Setting</th><th>Description</th><th>Default</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Usage</p></td><td><p>Message usage. Allowed value is <code>Receive from partners</code>.</p></td><td></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Format</p></td><td><p>Format of the message. Allowed value is <code>JSON</code> or <code>XML</code>.</p></td><td></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Schema</p></td><td><p>Imported JSON or XSD schema file for the message type.</p></td><td></td><td><p>No</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Message type identifier</p></td><td><div><div><p>Message type identifier value used in the dynamic message routing of inbound B2B transactions:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>If a schema is associated with the message type, Partner Manager automatically generates the message type identifier from the schema filename.<br><br>You can modify this value.</p></li><li><p>If a schema is not assciated with the message type, you must enter the message type identifier manually.</p></li></ul></div><div><p>Your partner must pass the message type identifier as follows when sending inbound messages:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>If you receive the payload via an HTTP-based endpoint, your partner must pass the message type identifier as the relative path in the HTTP request.</p></li><li><p>If you receive the payload via AS2 or a file-based endpoint protocol such as SFTP or FTP, your partner must prefix the message type identifier to the filename (<code>&lt;message-type-id&gt;_&lt;filename&gt;</code>).</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Reference identifiers and attributes mapping</p></td><td><div><div><p>Uploaded DataWeave map that extracts the reference identifiers, business key, and custom messaging attributes from the payload received from the partner:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>The extracted reference identifier values are used for dynamic routing into the appropriate message flow.</p></li><li><p>The extracted custom attributes enable you to have visibility into the lifecycle of your B2B workflow. For more information, see <a href="use-custom-attributes">Using Custom Messaging Attributes with Message Types</a>.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## X12

Use the following settings to define X12 as the format for the Receive from Partners message type:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Setting</th><th>Description</th><th>Default</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Usage</p></td><td><p>Message usage. Allowed value is <code>Receive from partners</code>.</p></td><td></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Format</p></td><td><p>Format of the message. Allowed value is <code>X12</code>.</p></td><td></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Version</p></td><td><p>X12 version, such as <code>3010</code> or <code>5020</code>.</p></td><td></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Message type</p></td><td><p>X12 transaction set number, such as <code>850</code> (purchase order) or <code>214</code>( for (shipment status).</p></td><td></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Reference identifiers and attributes mapping</p></td><td><div><div><p>Uploaded DataWeave map that extracts the reference identifiers, business key, and attribute from the payload received from the partner:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>The extracted reference identifier values are used for dynamic routing into the appropriate message flow.</p></li><li><p>The extracted custom attributes enable you to have visibility into the lifecycle of your B2B workflow. For more information, see <a href="use-custom-attributes">Using Custom Messaging Attributes with Message Types</a>.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td></td><td><p>No</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Schema</p></td><td><div><div><p>EDI schema for the message type. Options include:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><strong>Use standard X12-&lt;version&gt;</strong><br>Applies the standard X12 schema for the version and message type configured.</p></li><li><p><strong>Customize</strong><br>Enables you to import a customized <a href="../../x12-edi-connector/latest/x12-edi-schema-language-reference">EDI schema language</a> file if you must make adjustments to the standard X12 schema.</p><div><p>When using the customized EDI schema, you can assign a custom name to the message type definition, for example, <code>X12-7050-204-PartnerName</code>.</p></div></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Use standard X12-&lt;version&gt;</p></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

For an example that shows how to customize an X12 schema, see
[Customize EDI X12 Schema for B2B Integrations](https://www.mulesoft.com/exchange/works.integration/b2b-x12-custom-schema-example)
in Anypoint Exchange.

## See Also

- [Message Types](document-types)
- [Creating Message Types](partner-manager-create-message-type)
- [Deleting Message Types](delete-message-types)
---

# Send to Partners Message Type Settings

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

The Send to Partners message type defines the way in which Anypoint Partner Manager transforms the
outbound transactions sent from your organization to your partner. You can define the CSV, EDIFACT,
JSON, X12, or XML format for a Send to Partners message type.

## CSV

Use the following settings to define CSV as the format for the Send to Partners message type:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Setting</th><th>Description</th><th>Default</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Usage</p></td><td><p>Message usage. Allowed value is <code>Send to Partners</code>.</p></td><td></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Format</p></td><td><p>Format of the message. Allowed value is <code>CSV</code>.</p></td><td></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## EDIFACT

Use the following settings to define EDIFACT as the format for the Send to Partners message type:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Setting</th><th>Description</th><th>Default</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Usage</p></td><td><p>Message usage. Allowed value is <code>Send to partners</code>.</p></td><td></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Format</p></td><td><p>Format of the message. Allowed value is <code>EDIFACT</code>.</p></td><td></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Version</p></td><td><p>EDIFACT version, such as <code>d03b</code> or <code>d12a</code>.</p></td><td></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Message type</p></td><td><p>EDIFACT message type, such as <code>ORDERS</code>, <code>DESADV</code>, or <code>INVOIC</code>.</p></td><td></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Schema</p></td><td><div><div><p>EDI schema for the message type. Options include:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><strong>Use standard EDIFACT-&lt;version&gt;-&lt;message-type&gt;</strong><br>Applies the standard EDIFACT schema for the version and message type configured.</p></li><li><p><strong>Customize</strong><br>Enables you to import a customized <a href="../../x12-edi-connector/latest/x12-edi-schema-language-reference">EDI schema language</a> file if you must make adjustments to the standard EDIFACT schema.</p><div><p>When using a customized EDI schema, you can assign a custom name to the message type definition, for example, <code>EDIFACT-d97a-ORDERS-&lt;PartnerName&gt;</code>.</p></div></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Use standard EDIFACT- &lt;version&gt;</p></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## JSON or XML

Use the settings in the table to define JSON or XML as the format for the Receive from Partners
message type.

If you process cXML messages with <DOCTYPE> tags, you must set the `minMuleVersion` to 4.5.0 or
higher and upgrade your runtime environment. For more information, see Partner Manager deployment
settings for [CloudHub](cloudhub-deployment-settings),
[CloudHub 2.0](cloudhub2-deployment-settings), and [Runtime Fabric](rtf-deployment-settings).

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Setting</th><th>Description</th><th>Default</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Usage</p></td><td><p>Message usage. Allowed value is <code>Send to partners</code>.</p></td><td></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Format</p></td><td><p>Format of the message. Allowed value is <code>JSON</code> or <code>XML</code>.</p></td><td></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Schema</p></td><td><p>Imported JSON or XSD schema file for the message type.</p></td><td></td><td><p>No</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## X12

Use the following settings to define X12 as the format for the Send to Partners message type:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Setting</th><th>Description</th><th>Default</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Usage</p></td><td><p>Message usage. Allowed value is <code>Send to partners</code>.</p></td><td></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Format</p></td><td><p>Format of the message. Allowed value is <code>X12</code>.</p></td><td></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Version</p></td><td><p>X12 version, such as <code>3010</code> or <code>5040</code>.</p></td><td></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Message type</p></td><td><p>X12 transaction set number, such as <code>850</code> for a purchase order or <code>214</code> for the shipment status.</p></td><td></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Schema</p></td><td><div><div><p>EDI schema for the message type. Options include:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><strong>Use standard X12-&lt;version&gt;-&lt;message-type&gt;</strong><br>Applies the standard X12 schema for the version and message type configured.</p></li><li><p><strong>Customize</strong><br>Enables you to import a customized <a href="../../x12-edi-connector/latest/x12-edi-schema-language-reference">EDI schema language</a> file if you must make adjustments to the standard X12 schema.</p><div><p>When using the customized EDI schema, you can assign a custom name to the message type definition, for example, <code>X12-7050-204-PartnerName</code>.</p></div></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Use standard X12-&lt;version&gt;</p></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

For an example that shows how to customize an X12 schema, see
[Customize EDI X12 Schema for B2B Integrations](https://www.mulesoft.com/exchange/works.integration/b2b-x12-custom-schema-example)
in Anypoint Exchange.

## See Also

- [Message Types](document-types)
- [Creating Message Types](partner-manager-create-message-type)
- [Deleting Message Types](delete-message-types)
